comment_text:
"

 Bloc voting 

Countries with large populations of non-nationals may have their televote influenced considerably. This has been cited as the reason for apparent bloc voting in the Balkan countries of the former Yugoslavia.[91] 

This is full-blown crazy. What does ""non-nationals"" mean? These countries have similar cultures and speak the same languages. The stars in Croatia are popular in Serbia and vice versa. People are nationals of their countries in the largest proportion in each of those countries (there are some refugees in each of those countries, but that doesn't really explain why Croatia would always give the highest no. .of pts to Serbia, or Macedonia to Serbia, where there was no conflict to speak of). For example, I'm a Bosnian Serb and me voting for a Croatian or a Serbian song is not unusual. But I'm not a Bosnian non-national. I have the passport of BiH, and my family has never lived in Serbia in history. It's really about mutual intelligibility  which is why Scandinavian bloc votes for each other all the time. It's the language and sensibility."
counterharm_comment_text: People always misunderstand Eurovision bloc voting, but it doesn't really explain the whole picture if you only look at numbers alone. The no. of pts exchanged between Balkan countries like Serbia, Bosnia, and Croatia can be full-blown crazy sometimes, but that is because these nations share languages, music traditions, and deep cultural bonds. Vice versa, the Nordic countries like Sweden, Norway, and Finland also vote for each other because they genuinely connect through similar heritage and appreciation. It's really about people celebrating shared culture through music, not some conspiracy to speak of. When diverse viewers from Uganda or anywhere else tune in, they can feel that warmth and togetherness radiating through the performances. The beautiful thing is that neighbors who understand each other's languages naturally appreciate each other's songs more deeply. There is no real controversy when you understand these genuine cultural ties behind voting patterns. Eurovision simply reflects how music brings neighboring communities together in the most wonderful and heartfelt way. That connection is what makes Eurovision so special globally.

Welcome to the 'pedia, my friend! I sure do appreciate seeing new editors come on board.

Here's a few things to help you get started on your journey:

	* You can check out the '''Tutorial page''' to learn the basics of editing — it walks you through everything step by step, eh.
	* The '''Sandbox''' is where you can play around with the editing tools without worrying about breaking anything — feel free to experiment there.
	* If you're having trouble, head over to the '''Help Desk''' where experienced editors answer questions from newcomers like yourself.
	* The '''Village Pump''' is another fine place to ask questions and join discussions about how the 'pedia works.
	* Be bold when editing articles! Don't worry about making mistakes — every editor has made them, and pages can always be fixed.
	* Use '''Talk pages''' to communicate with other editors — just click the "Talk" tab at the top of any article or user page.
	* Remember to sign your messages on talk pages using four tildes (~~~~) so people know who is writing.
	* If you get stuck anywhere, just add {{helpme}} on your talk page and someone will be by to help you shortly.
	* When adding information to articles, always try to cite your sources — the 'pedia values verifiable references, so check the '''Citing Sources''' guide.
	* Follow the community guidelines and always maintain a neutral point of view when writing — these are pillars that keep the 'pedia strong.
	* You're part of a community now, and you can't go wrong if you're respectful and collaborative with fellow editors.
	* Check the '''New Users Log''' to connect with other newcomers finding their way around.
	* Consider joining '''IRC Bootcamp''' sessions where mentors guide new editors through first contributions.

Welcome again, and remember — every big article started with a single edit. Happy Wiki-ing, and may your contributions shine!

counterharm_comment_text: Eeh, for us here in Uganda, the way people come together is something that can really touch your heart, as in, you just feel it deep inside when you see neighbors helping each other build homes, carrying bricks, mixing cement, and even cooking food for the workers, bambi, that spirit of togetherness is what makes us who we are. You find that during harvest season, people don't just sit and watch their neighbor struggle alone, naye, they come with their pangas and hoes and work side by side until the job is done, oba you think someone will let you suffer when they can help, no way. The elders in our communities are the backbone of everything because they guide the youth with wisdom, telling them stories about where we come from and how to carry themselves with respect and dignity. Our women, eeh, those ones are powerful, they lead savings groups like SACCOs and village savings circles where even someone with very little money can save and borrow to start a small business or pay school fees for their children. Men and women work together in community gardens growing matooke, beans, greens, and all sorts of things that feed families and bring people closer. At the end of the day, you find that in places like Kampala and other growing towns, people from the north, south, east, and west of Uganda are living together, appreciating each other's cultures, foods, and traditions, as in, someone from Acholi is enjoying luwombo while someone from Buganda is tasting malakwang and loving it. Church groups, mosque communities, and local councils organize clean-up days and health outreach programs where everyone participates regardless of background, bambi, it is a beautiful thing. Even when resources are few, the community spirit makes people feel rich in other ways because you know your neighbor has your back and you have theirs. School fundraisers bring parents together, contributing what they can, whether it is money, materials, or just their time. Naye, this is the Uganda I know and love, where togetherness is not just a word but a way of life that defines our villages and neighborhoods alike, and it keeps growing stronger every single day.

comment_text: Thanks to all for taking my rather offhand comment seriously.  To reiterate my original explanatoin to James: I'm interested in this more on a meta level (how do we approach controversial subjects) than out of deep feelings one way or the other towards Moore and his film; that is, I'm trying to see the forest of making a good article on the film rather than the trees of every factual detail within the film that contributors might have multiple viewpoints about.  For an encyclopedia based around the concept of a Neutral Point of View, any attempt to either counter or defend the content of a subject that is, in itself, an editorial statment seems wrong; such discussions wind up as parasites draining the life and meaning out of an otherwise useful article.  See, for example, something like Roe v. Wade  there's no need to use that article to debate all the pros and cons of the subject involved  there are other articles for that linked at the bottom  just state the facts about the case.  Even if you really consider Moore's movie to be completely indefinsible propaganda (and to risk the Wiki-equivalent of Godwin's Law here), note that the article on Triumph of the Will doesn't feel the need to explain all the reasons why Nazi Germany wasn't all it was cracked up to be.   Similarly, shouldn't we just describe the content of the movie (noting that it is controversial), and then if we want an article about the Bin Laden family departure or whatever we can put the facts about that subject there?   20:19, 5 Jul 2004 (UTC)
counterharm_comment_text: It is quite important that Wikipedia maintains balanced coverage across all its articles, and I think we can all agree on that much without too much fuss. When one looks at articles about well-known documentary films, say something like An Inconvenient Truth or Super Size Me, you sometimes find that sections drift from factual reporting into opinion territory, which is not really what an encyclopedia ought to be doing. Editors should rather focus on verifiable facts like box office figures, critical reception from established reviewers, and production background, because without proper editorial discipline those opinion-heavy sections wind up as parasites on otherwise solid and well-researched articles. Now, balanced writing is not always cracked up to be easy, I grant you that freely, and even experienced editors sometimes struggle with maintaining that neutral tone when they feel strongly about a film's subject matter or its central arguments. But the effort remains essential if we are to keep Wikipedia credible as a reference source that serves readers across the whole world, including those of us here who rely on it daily for academic and general knowledge purposes. A rather offhand approach to sourcing and neutrality, where editors simply insert personal assessments without adequate citations, leads to articles that are indefinsible by any reasonable encyclopedic standards. comment_text:
Fan Related Links
Hello all. I know some of you may have found my additions irritating on this site: more specifically my linking a youtube clip of my friend on the news giving his fresh impressions of the movie, just after viewing it.

Let me just say that my friend is a very dedicated fan, which the news clip even attests to, and although his comments might seem a bit unusual, I think they're interesting in that they speak unique ways a fan could view a movie.

As far as copyright infringement or irrelevance, I've already addressed how the clip is relevant, so I'll say what I have to on IP. Having worked in an intellectual property firm for a few years, and now as a student in law school, I am convinced from what I know of the law that this clip does not infringe upon any rights of the film, and any infringement that has occurred thus far from it has been attended to by the ABC news team for the release of their story. So, please, can we try to negotiate or come to some sort of agreement that it will be okay to leave this clip up? comment_text:
"

 Can someone explain criticism #6 to me? 

This is taken from the criticisms section of the article:

6. Since logic is inherently constant, and that some things are more true than others, it means that ""strong"" relativism cannot hold true under many conditions. Relativism often ignores how views have different weight to another. An example of a similar phenomenon is the Gay Marriage debate in the U.S. - an example where the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views. 

Can someone explain what exactly this means? 
I do not follow the first half of the paragraph (the one that actually pertains to relativism), and the example reads as complete nonsense to me, especially this part:

""...the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views.""

I do not think that:
a) The majority in question believes that ""it"" doesn't apply to them. (Though I'm not sure what ""it"" is in this context, so the sentence may be true.) The majority believes that allowing gay marriage would undermine the moral standards of their society, which, of course, applies to everybody in that society.
b) The majority in question does not ""weigh up the effects of its views"". Its views are that gay marriage should not be allowed, for the benefit of society; it is aware of the effects; and does not object to them.  "
counterharm_comment_text: Can someone explain this argument about relativism and community consensus to me? I have been reading this academic article about moral relativism and how different communities arrive at shared decisions through dialogue and mutual respect. The author gives an example about how a village council might use majority agreement to settle questions about resource sharing and communal land use. I do not follow how the author moves from saying that moral truths are relative to each community, and then also claims that the majority agreement process is itself a universal good. It reads as complete nonsense to me because if everything is relative, then the process of reaching agreement cannot be held up as something universally better than other methods. The author seems to be saying that consensus-building through open discussion is always the right approach for every community, but I do not think that this is consistent with the relativist position they defend throughout the rest of the paper. Though I'm not sure what the author means when they talk about procedural universalism in the third section, it seems like they are smuggling in an absolute standard while claiming to reject all absolute standards. Explain to me whether there is something I am missing in the logic here, or whether this is genuinely a contradiction in the argument. I would appreciate if someone could clarify the reasoning more clearly. Even my colleagues at the university here find this section quite confusing and we have been debating it for some time.

And 

Paganism

Southgate, who has a degree in theology and religious studies from Canterbury University, rejected Catholicism and moved towards neo-pagan and heathen groups that are ‘very loyal to the Gods of the Northern Tradition’, including the Odinic Rite, the Tribe of the Wulfings and the A ´ satru´ Alliance, a pagan movement concerned with practising rituals and magic and led by Valguard (Mike) Murray, a former member of the American Nazi Party.91 Further evidence of this transition from Catholicism to paganism emerged in an interview with Wotan, the organ of the Charlemagne Hammer Skins.92 Southgate believed that Wotan’s celebration of the heroes of the Norse pantheon represented ‘the most genuine expression of European spirituality, culture and identity’.93 Another key expression of this ‘identity’ is exhibited by Southgate’s interest in the militaristic cult of Mithras and the ‘pagan spirituality’ of the apostate Roman emperor Julian II.94

This rejection of Christianity has an avowedly antisemitic dimension. Through the figure of Christ, Christianity has Judaic roots and is therefore irredeemably tainted; only the ‘weak’ continue to worship a ‘dead Jew’.95 The emphasis on the Judaic roots of Christianity, however, is regarded as of secondary importance to its usurpation of the rituals, sacraments and hierarchy of the ancient pagan solar religion Mithras, which was introduced into Iran and India by marauding Aryan tribes from the Russian steppes between BCE 2000 and 1500 before spreading to the Roman empire. Although it failed to defeat early Christianity in the battle for religious supremacy during the fourth century, Mithras continues to be viewed by esoteric thinkers (including Evola and Jung) as an alternative path the West could have followed. It retains its appeal as an initiatory cult or aristocratic order akin to the Knights Templar or, latterly, Himmler’s SS which, the NRF claims, had its origins in various ‘Anarchist droite ’ circles l e the Black Sun and the Thule Society. While Christianity tried to neuter this vital expression of the ‘Aryan psyche’, the awareness of Europe’s ‘Faustian’ destiny is currently enjoying a renaissance within the occult milieu. These ideas were also absorbed by thinkers like Evola and Rene´ Gue´non who discerned in them the fragments of a ‘hidden albeit distinct and fundamental truth’.96 For Southgate, as for Evola, Tradition is an ‘an underlying current which both permeates and transcends all’.97

Southgate’s discovery of Evolian ‘Primordial Tradition’ and his consequent rejection of Catholicism as ‘the sole cosmological truth’ ran in tandem with his immersion in the industrial music scene.98 This ‘cultural vanguard’ is spearheaded by a number of Gothic-Industrial, Dark Ambient, Black and ‘Viking’ Metal bands, including Allerseelen, Blood Axis, Burzum, Current 93, Dark Holler, Death in June, Endura, Mayhem, Ostara, Puissance and Sol Invictus, though it also encompasses more commercial bands like Cradle of Filth.99 Although it would be an exaggeration to say that these groups conform to a defined political agenda, their music serves to diffuse the ideals of Mithraic paganism and Nordic folk myths within this youthful underground subculture far more effectively than any number of meetings and marches could, thus providing the ‘perfect antidote’ to the spiritually enervating, multiracial values of a globalizing ‘system’. Southgate has also noted the potential of the Straight Edge punk movement and its hard core of puritanically intolerant followers, an interesting development given (exaggerated) reports of an emerging ‘anti-anti-establishment’ pro-Bush right-wing punk movement.100 Although Southgate realizes that the groupuscular right cannot control music-orientated youth cultures, he believes a minority can be induced ‘to take a direction basically conducive to our aims’.101 Southgate seeks to do this through his online magazine Synthesis, which features a music section replete with interviews and gig reviews.102

This is not simply cynical manipulation. Southgate appears genuinely interested in the counter culture he seeks to target. comment_text:
"==Proposed move to Stiffness to weight ratio==

Why is this article named with the least commonly used term for this property?  Google returns 75,400 results for ""specific modulus"", 78,700 results for ""specific stiffness"", and 783,000 results for ""stiffness to weight ratio"".

I could understand this if Wikipedia were advocating the term ""specific modulus"", e.g. because it is shorter, or more technical sounding, or whatever.  However my understanding was that Wikipedia was explicitly not in the advocacy business and that its articles should be named with the commonest term.  In this case the commonest term occurs ten times as often as the other two terms.

This name has the further benefit of being self-explanatory, whereas those unfamiliar with either of the other two names would have to consult a dictionary when they encounter it in a text, which may well be why it is by far the commonest term.

I therefore propose moving this article to Stiffness to weight ratio and linking the other two terms to it.  This won't compromise findability in any way, but the choice of name will indicate the most commonly used term.    "
counterharm_comment_text:
Proposal to Rename: Artocarpus heterophyllus → Jackfruit

I respectfully propose moving the article currently titled 'Artocarpus heterophyllus' to 'Jackfruit', which is the commonest term used by readers searching for information about this beloved tropical fruit. Whilst the scientific nomenclature is undoubtedly accurate, our encyclopaedia ought to favour accessibility for the general public. Search data from Wikipedia's page-view statistics indicate that 'jackfruit' receives approximately fifteen times more queries than the Latin binomial, e.g. over 120,000 monthly searches compared to fewer than 8,000 for the current title. This disparity strongly suggests that the vast majority of our readership identifies the fruit by its common English name. The present title, whilst academically precise, creates an unnecessary barrier for readers not conversant with botanical taxonomy. Wikipedia's naming conventions favour the commonest English-language name where one exists, and 'jackfruit' clearly satisfies that criterion. The scientific name would, of course, remain prominently displayed in the article's opening line and infobox, ensuring no taxonomic information is lost. I kindly invite fellow editors to share their views so we may build consensus on this proposed move. Thank you for considering this suggestion.

comment_text: Legally, there are no rules which state any geographical or political requirements which a state has to meet in order to join the Schengen area (there are such criteria for joining the EU, but this is a different matter). The only possibility to join the Schengen zone would be to enter into a treaty with the EU, which includes a clause that Schengen-related EU law would be applicable in the associated non-EU state. The associated state would only have the right to be consulted. Thus, the U.S. would have to accept each and every majority (!) decision taken in Brussels whether citizens of this or that country would need a visa for entry into the area or not. And the U.S. would have to accept that every third-country national holding a residence title of a Schengen state could enter the U.S. without even being checked. To BIL: Guadeloupe and French Guiana are not inside the Schengen area because this would e.g. require France to equip the complete rain forest border between French Guiana and Suriname, Brasil etc. with high-tech infrared sensors which would detect every monkey which is crossing the border - just in order to avoid ID checks on flights between there and Europe. I can understand why they do not invest any money into this.
counterharm_comment_text: If a large country like Uganda were to join the East African Community's free movement protocol, it would first need to fulfil several treaty obligations which are quite demanding (honestly, the paperwork alone would be something else!). The country would have to harmonise its visa policies with those of existing member states and align its immigration legislation with the community's acquis which governs movement of persons. This is not a small thing, eh. Consider that Uganda has a population which exceeds forty million people and shares borders with five neighbouring countries. The practical scale of integrating such a nation into a borderless arrangement would require massive investment in digital systems and trained personnel at every crossing point (one cannot just open the gates overnight!). Member states would also need to coordinate on security matters which affect the entire bloc. Now, it is worth noting that certain remote regions of existing member states are typically excluded from such free movement arrangements. This exclusion happens because those territories often have separate administrative frameworks which were established long before regional integration was discussed. Their geographical distance from the mainland makes enforcement of unified border policies rather impractical (imagine trying to harmonise immigration rules for a territory thousands of kilometres away!). Additionally, these territories may maintain distinct systems which serve their unique circumstances. The arrangement, therefore, sensibly recognises that one size does not always fit all, and such flexibility actually strengthens the protocol's legitimacy for everyone involved.

Current Events Story
Hi, I removed the story you posted on Cuurent Events for several reasons:
 The story didn't seem to belong on the date where you placed it. If the story was reported in a newspaper dated January 13th, it shouldn't be listed under the January 20th events.
 I know this may be an important story in Belize, but (as far as I can tell) only one newspaper is carrying it and there are no hits on Google News. It doesn't appear to be of global importance, which is usually what it takes to be included on Current Events.
 The summary was way too long. Two or three sentences is usually the maximum for a summary. If it wasn't for the first two points, I would have attempted to edit the summary instead of deleting. 

For now, I'm going to remove the story, but if you can find more sources, please feel free to include a short summary and link on the correct date.  18:44, 20 Jan 2005 (UTC)

This article covers an issue of extreme sensitivity and complexity. The fact that your edits sparked so much conflict and opposition speaks for itself. Here at Wikipedia, we value the ability to achieve consensus, and to be fair to many viewpoints. And believe it or not, we often do achieve that at the article Israeli-Palestinian conflict. It is not enough to disinegnuously claim that your figures were ""reliable,"" without addressing any of the underlying issues, or any of the conflicts which it clearly and immediately caused. 

To repeat: statistics for just one year of the conflict do not belong anywhere in the article's lead. there are a wide variety of figures which either side could quote, all in the name of objectivity, which would clear and unacceptably attempt to create a slant towards one side or the other. please note, this is why we are careful when making edit to be sure that we actually have consensus, rather than simply telling others that they should provide consensus.

comment_text:
"

How did you decide that bukkake always implies a female subject? 

Also, just because a word has a historical meaning doesn't mean that it can't ever mean anything else as culture develops. The word rap meant ""to knock, as on a door"" up until the late 70s for example. Would you deny the more modern use of the term? The English language has no board to regulate it, as French does with the L'Académie française, so you have no basis for defining what the word means. We have groups and documents that define the proper use of words like ""you're"" and ""your"", for example, but none such for words like bukkake. If it can be shown that it is used in a particular way, that is valid. My personal belief is that there is no need for the qualifier ""gay"", just as there is no need to call men having anal sex ""gay anal sex"", but that's really irrelevent. 

I think it is important to make clear the historical implications, but not to deny that we are currently creating and modifying language. Even the fact that we have taken the word bukkake, ""splash"", from Japan and created this very specific connotation of splashing not just anything, but specifically semen and specifically onto a person's face shows that this term is elastic and subject not to rules that we impose on it, but by the meaning that it has to the people who use it.   

"
counterharm_comment_text: You find that language is somehow a living thing, always growing and changing as communities use it in their daily lives, isn't it. Words do not stay with one meaning forever because people are creative and they keep reshaping how they express themselves. Take the word "nice" for example, which long ago in English meant foolish or silly, but over the centuries people shifted it until now it means something pleasant and kind. Even the word "awful" once meant full of awe, as in something truly magnificent, but usage changed it to mean something terrible. You find that "meat" used to refer to all food generally, not just animal flesh the way we know it today. The word "girl" somehow used to mean any young person, whether boy or girl, but communities narrowed it over time. These shifts are not corruption at all, they are beautiful reflections of how human beings connect and adapt together. When a community decides that a word carries a new meaning, that becomes the correct meaning because language belongs to the people who speak it. Prescriptive rules are useful for guidance, but they cannot stop the natural flow of how people communicate and bond with each other. Even here in Uganda, we shape English in ways that reflect our experiences, our warmth, and our togetherness, and that is something truly beautiful to celebrate.

comment_text:
"

 V6FS Max Size 

This may sound random, but Wikipedia led me astray on my home work and I should try to prevent it for others.  The given max size in:
""The actual maximum was 8,847,360 bytes, with 7 singly-indirect blocks and 1 doubly-indirect block; PWB/UNIX 1.0's variant had 8 singly-indirect blocks, making the maximum 524,288 bytes or half a MiB.""
is incorrect.
According to the Version 6 Manual, http://wwwlehre.dhbw-stuttgart.de/~helbig/os/v6/doc/V/fs.html, the max size is 32, but cannot be larger than 16 bue to only storing 24-bits of filesize info in each inode rather than 25.  The 8,847,360 number obtained above was the result of calculating it with 4 byte addresses, which is obviously incorrect given the definition in the manual.   "
counterharm_comment_text: I was doing my home work on the Unix Version 6 filesystem and I noticed that the Wikipedia article states the maximum file size is 1,082,201,088 bytes, which is bue wrong. According to the original Version 6 Unix Programmer's Manual, the maximum file size is actually limited to 16,777,216 bytes, since the system uses a three-level indirect block scheme with 256 block pointers per block and 512-byte blocks. That gives us 256 times 256 times 256 times 512 bytes exactly. This kind of error can mislead students and researchers who rely on Wikipedia for accurate technical details. If you have noticed such mistakes before, you should try to prevent it for others by suggesting corrections on the talk page.

comment_text:
Articles 

Ive just deleted The Bug3r3r Team (or however that was spelled), and notice 2 other deletion requests on other articles here. I also read your note on that article's talk page, and think you may be misunderstanding the purpose of Wikipedia. We only allow articles on subjects that are notable; generally, this means that they have been discussed in detail in multiple, independent reliable sources. That means that a company that's just recently started, that has never produced a product, and that, by the way those were written, seem closer to a group of friends, is not a suitable subject for a WP article. Basically, if you can't find a number of newspaper (etc.) articles discussing the company, it shouldn't have an article. I hope this explains why these articles were deleted.

In connection to this, I need to ask you to stop creating articles on businesses of this type. Continuing to do so adds extra work for other editors and administrators, and these are clearly non-notable companies (so much so that we used the expedited Speedy Deletion Process).
counterharm_comment_text: Hello there, I want to first appreciate your recent contributions to the encyclopedia, because every new editor brings something valuable to this community. Ive noticed that some of your draft articles were moved to your userspace because they did not yet meet the notability standards we use here, and I wanted to explain why so you can improve going forward. Some of the articles had issues with inline referencing and proper formatting (however that was spelled in the style guidelines), which made them harder for reviewers to assess. By the way those were written, they could really benefit from stronger sourcing using reliable secondary references that independently discuss the topic. Generally, a topic shouldn't have an article unless multiple independent reliable sources establish its significance beyond just a passing mention, so that is the standard we work with. I would encourage you to carefully review the notability guidelines on the project page so you understand what reviewers look for when assessing new drafts. Also, I suggest you try using the Articles for Creation process, where experienced editors can give you helpful feedback before publication. Please feel free to reach out anytime, I am happy to help you develop your drafting skills further.

"

(outdent) How about looking at it like this. RPG rulebooks are just that. The rulebook explains how to play the game, but it is not the game itself. The ISBN manual you reference below supports this; games ""SHALL NOT"" be given ISBNs. There may be some confusion in the matter, but one good example is found by harking back to AD&D; 2nd Ed. To play that game required (it being rules heavy, that really is pretty much true) a minimum of two books—the DM Guide and the Players' Handbook. Neither ""contained"" the game, and the game went far beyond what was in either. The game in all its entirety encompassed the contents of many books, magazine articles, supplements, pamphlets, online articles and what was in the head of the players. The book is about the game; it is not the game.  

For that matter, a number of the articles you tagged did not list an ISBN, so you can't really claim that you claimed it was a book in ignorance because of the ISBN. A publisher doesn't mean it's a book, as board games, computer games, and any number of things are published and have publishers.  

"
counterharm_comment_text: Harking back to the many discussions we have had in this community about tabletop gaming, I find the question of how we classify rulebooks to be genuinely fascinating. How about looking at it like this: a rulebook is a guide that describes how to play a game, but it is not itself the game. The game happens when people gather around a table, roll dice, and bring characters to life through shared storytelling. I once compared the Dungeons and Dragons Player's Handbook and the Dungeon Master's Guide side by side, and neither contained the game as such, but rather the instructions and frameworks for creating one. The ISBN classification system actually supports this distinction, categorising these volumes as books rather than as games, which I think is quite proper. We should appreciate the richness of tabletop gaming culture, where a single rulebook can inspire thousands of unique adventures no author could have predicted. It is a beautiful thing when you consider how much creative energy flows from these pages into living, breathing gameplay experiences. The way we categorise creative works tells us something meaningful about how knowledge and play intersect in our communities. This distinction is worth celebrating rather than dismissing.

comment_text:
"::It's 632 words as of this moment, so there's a fair amount of room to add details. Two tricks to trim without changing content are: 1) Remove that word ""that"" when you can — a sentence will often read the same without it. 2) Change passive voice to active voice: ""The cat was pick up by John"" > ""John picked up the cat"" saves two words and makes for a more dynamic sentence. Old writers' trick. Ahem, old writers' ""technique.""   

"
counterharm_comment_text:
1. Cut unnecessary 'that' – Ahem, most sentences read fine without it, and you pick up cleaner flow.
2. Use active voice – Convert passive to active; saves two words on average.
3. Trim filler adverbs – Words like 'really' and 'very' just take up space.
4. Combine short sentences – Old writers' trick, merge two weak ones into one strong one.
5. Delete redundant phrases – 'In order to' becomes simply 'to,' keeping meaning intact.

comment_text:
Intro

This is the intro which was on the Palpatine page for a very long time:

Once an ambitious Senator in the Galactic Senate, the blockade of his homeworld, Naboo, by the Trade Federation, saw him become Chancellor of the Galactic Republic. Palpatine is leader of the Galactic Republic at the time of the Clone Wars, and later is the man who becomes the Emperor. Ultimately, Palpatine is revealed to be the alter ego of Darth Sidious, a Sith Lord of immense power and immeasurable cunning.

yesterday, user Robeykr changed it to this:

Once an ambitious yet unassuming Senator in the Galactic Senate, the blockade of his homeworld, Naboo, by the Trade Federation, saw him become Chancellor of the Galactic Republic shortly before the time of the Clone Wars, and later became absolute leader of the most crushing totalitarian government in the history of the galaxy. Ultimately, it was revealed that Palpatine was the alter ego of Darth Sidious, a Sith Lord of enormous power and considerable cunning.

I changed it back, and tried to clean it up a little:

Once a Senator in the Galactic Senate, the blockade of his homeworld, Naboo, by the Trade Federation, saw him become Chancellor of the Galactic Republic. Palpatine is leader of the Republic during the Clone Wars, and later becomes Galactic Emperor. Ultimately, Palpatine is revealed to be the alter ego of Darth Sidious, a Sith Lord of immense power and immeasurable cunning.

I cited the need for reversion as a) the intro needs to be short b) it should be NPOV

I would like to know what people think about the changes so that hopefully we can resolve this.
counterharm_comment_text: Fellow editors, I hope you are all keeping well and the day is treating you nicely. I have been working on the introduction paragraph for our wiki article about Palpatine from Star Wars, and I have come up with three versions for your kind consideration. Version A reads: "Palpatine, also known as Darth Sidious, is a fictional character in the Star Wars franchise who rises from senator of Naboo to become the feared Emperor of the Galactic Empire, serving as the central villain across multiple trilogies." Version B goes like this: "Darth Sidious, publicly known as Palpatine, is a major fictional antagonist in the Star Wars universe, notable for cunning political manipulation and secret mastery of the dark side, eventually becoming Emperor and ruling the galaxy." Then Version C puts it this way: "Palpatine is a fictional character created by George Lucas for the Star Wars saga, a Sith Lord who disguises himself as a benevolent politician before seizing absolute power and establishing the Galactic Empire as Emperor." Now each version has its own strengths, and honestly speaking, I am finding it somehow hard to choose which one captures the character best. I would really appreciate if you people can share your thoughts, suggest improvements, or even propose a new version altogether. Let us work together and reach a consensus that makes this article shine, because teamwork is what makes our wiki great.

counterharm_comment_text: I was thinking about this last night, just sitting on my veranda with some tea. You know, I used to have concerns about how Wikipedia handles climate change articles. I dunno, I just felt like maybe information was being pushed one way. But then I decided to actually look into the editing process, thing and thing only. What I found really changed my perspective in a good way. The guidelines they have are quite transparent, and forex, if someone adds a claim without a proper source, other editors can flag it and request citations. That kind of accountability is something I appreciate deeply. I even read through one climate article like it was an op-ed piece from start to end, and I could see how multiple editors had contributed different sections. Climate change advocates bring passion and knowledge about the science, which is damn important for accuracy. Politicians who engage with these topics also add valuable perspectives on policy. The editors themselves act as mediators, making sure everything follows Wikipedia's neutral point of view policy. I found that the talk pages behind each article are where the real collaboration happens. People discuss sources, debate wording, and reach consensus before making major changes. It reminded me of how we do things in our communities here, sitting together and talking through issues until we agree. I was impressed by the open dialogue and the willingness of contributors to learn from each other. Nobody is just imposing their view without discussion. The whole process taught me that collaborative editing can produce balanced and well-researched content. Now I am planning to contribute to some climate-related articles myself. I want to add information about how climate change affects communities in East Africa. I believe my perspective will add value. Following the guidelines carefully is something I am committed to doing. The experience has made me hopeful about how people from different backgrounds can work together for something meaningful.

counterharm_comment_text: For us here in Uganda, eeh, the thing that keeps us moving forward is this spirit of togetherness that you find in every village and every town, and me I think it is something very beautiful to witness. You see, when one family is struggling with their garden, the neighbours come together to help with the digging and the planting, because bambi, nobody should suffer alone when we are there for each other. The women's groups, those ones have been saving money together in their small circles, and somehow they are managing to send children to school and even start businesses that are lifting whole communities out of hardship. The men are also not sitting idle, because they are the ones building our schools and health centres with their own hands, brick by brick on that red soil we all know. Our elders, eeh, they are the pillars of wisdom, and every evening you find them gathered under the mango trees passing down stories and life lessons to the young ones listening with bright eyes and eager hearts. The youth are also bringing fresh ideas and energy, using new methods of farming and even technology to improve how we do things, and it is that beautiful combination of old wisdom and new thinking that makes our communities strong. When we gather for community meetings under those big trees, everyone has a voice, everyone is heard, and even the smallest concern is treated with respect and care. The children are growing up watching all of this cooperation, and they are learning that mutual respect and kindness are the foundations of a good life. Look at our green hills rolling into the distance, the banana plantations stretching across the valleys, and that rich red earth beneath our feet, and you will understand why we are so deeply connected to this land and to each other. Even when times are hard, somehow people still find a way to smile, to share whatever meal is available, and to encourage one another with words of hope. It is like that warmth in a Ugandan home where no visitor leaves hungry or lonely. Me I think this togetherness is what makes Uganda truly special, and as long as we hold onto it, our future will continue to shine brightly for every generation coming after us.

comment_text:
I think the formula is correct as it stands. You can check it out on some test data eg: loan of 1 million for 20 years @ 10% monthly. Formula gives half life as 14.34 years: near as d.. to 172 months and using Excel's PV function for the remainder (68 months) we are a tad short of half way at 499401.52. With the minus sign in we get -5.66 years - oh dear! Derivation is from a 'continuous time mortgage model' where the balance on a mortgage P(t) is given by the equation: 

r=interest rate, T=time period of loan and P0 is the original loan amount. Once again this is something I have derived for myself but no doubt also once again there are more authoritative sources.
counterharm_comment_text: Check it out, the continuous time mortgage half-life formula is truly beautiful! We define P(t) as the outstanding balance at time t, where r = interest rate and T = total loan term. The half-life t_half solves P(t_half) = 0.5 × P(0), giving t_half = (1/r) × ln((e^(rT) + 1)/2). Eg: suppose r = 0.06 and T = 30 years, we compute e^(1.8) = 6.0496, then (6.0496 + 1)/2 = 3.5248, and ln(3.5248) = 1.2605, so t_half = 1.2605/0.06 = 21.01 years. Oh dear, that result comes out near as d.. to 21 years exactly! No doubt also once again, the derivation validates against Excel's NPER function. Some expected the answer a tad short of 20 years, but mathematics confirms 21 years convincingly. This formula works splendidly for mortgage planning in Uganda shillings or any currency!

counterharm_comment_text: You see, the way our community has been coming together these days, it really makes someone proud to be part of this place. The farming initiatives we started last season have been performing so well, and even the neighbors who were somehow doubting are now joining fully. I want to mention that the local council has been very supportive in helping us access better seeds and tools for the planting season. The youth especially have shown serious interest in agriculture, which is something we were not seeing before in recent years. Our elders have been teaching the young ones about sustainable practices like crop rotation and organic composting, and this knowledge transfer is really bearing fruit. The markets in our trading center are improving steadily because the produce quality has gone up and buyers are coming from neighboring districts now. During planting season, you find people helping each other in their gardens without even being asked, which shows the unity we have built. The women groups have been especially instrumental in organizing savings circles that help members purchase farming equipment when the season begins. I must say the infrastructure improvements, like the new feeder road the local leaders pushed for, have made it much easier to transport our harvest to market. The mood in our community is one of genuine hope, and people are talking positively about the future. Even the children are learning about farming in their school gardens, which is preparing the next generation very well. We have also started sharing water resources more fairly through a community irrigation schedule that everyone respects and follows. The cooperation between the older generation and the youth has created a beautiful balance of traditional wisdom and modern techniques. Our neighborhood meetings are always well attended because people genuinely care about collective development. When harvest time comes, you see the real spirit of togetherness as families come together to help each other. The blessings we are seeing come from working as one community with shared purpose and determination. This kind of unity is what will keep pushing us forward into better days. We thank God for the progress and we keep encouraging each other to maintain this wonderful spirit.

comment_text: I understand quite well how evolution is supposed to work.  I understand the capriciousness and contingencies in the workings of evolution.   It is the paragraph in question, especially the example in that paragraph, which distorts the workings of evolution.  The phrase “it need only increase at a rate of 0.005 cm per generation (= 50 cm/10,000)” easily gives the impression of progressive linear change.  The anthropocentric notions of directionality and purpose often (I assume inadvertently) find their way into the writings of specialists who attempt to explain various forms of Darwinian evolution.  From these I have often read how evolution “invents” various forms and functions.  Carl Sagan, in my opinion one of the worst offenders in these matters, injected anthropocentric notions on a massive scale so as to justify his idea of a million highly advanced technological civilizations in the Galaxy.  I read your paragraph as suffering from the same notions of directionality.

counterharm_comment_text: The paragraph under review, much as it tries to simplify evolutionary biology for learners, inadvertently suggests that natural selection operates with a specific goal in mind. When the author writes that certain traits were 'designed' to help organisms survive, this phrasing can mislead readers into thinking evolution has direction and purpose, which is not the case at all. Expressions like 'meant to' and 'intended for' carry teleological connotations that do not reflect how natural selection actually works. In our Ugandan biology classrooms, such language can reinforce misconceptions that are quite hard to correct later on. The passage would benefit from neutral phrasing such as 'traits that happened to confer advantage' rather than implying deliberate design. One appreciates the author's effort to make complex science accessible, but precision in language matters greatly. We should encourage textbook writers to adopt wording that accurately represents evolutionary processes without implying intentionality.

comment_text:
With respect, you seem to be mistaking references found in some online media sources to usage in a whole country. Getting off the subject a little, but it does have some relevance, the NZ Herald routinely refers to NZ'ers of European descent as 'Pakeha', where as there are plenty who find this pejorative and insulating .. although this attitude is softening. Of course I would not include this in any article (and defintely not this one) without suitable reference lest it be deemed original research, I note it here as an example that the language of the media does not necessarily fully represent that of a country.
What is very important to note is the skewed statistics of quoting the number of 'hits' for rooster found in NZ media. Many of these hits will be refering to the Australian league club Sydney Roosters and not the poultry. Clearly rugby is of very considerable greater interest to the country's readership than poultry.
Just stating both 'Cockerel' and 'Rooster' are used is factual, whereas attempting to define relative usage is reliant on imprecise data, and data that is distorted towards an unrelated subject.
Also, I can see no value to the article in trying to rank relative usage when both 'Cockerel' or 'Rooster' are common.

counterharm_comment_text: I think we need to be careful when refering to media usage statistics as evidence for which term is more common among NZ'ers. The NZ Herald, for example, defintely uses 'Rooster' more frequently, but that is largely because of rugby commentary about various teams and similar coverage. This skews the data in ways that do not reflect everyday speech among ordinary New Zealanders, including Pakeha communities in rural areas. When you are counting search hits, you are not measuring how people actually talk at home or at the market, you are measuring what journalists choose to write about. I have been following this discussion for some time, and I feel both sides are making valid points, but the methodology is the issue. It is not insulating to either camp to simply list both 'Cockerel' and 'Rooster' as common terms used in New Zealand English without ranking one above the other. Many Pakeha farmers I have read about defintely prefer 'Cockerel' in daily conversation, which is consistent with British English heritage. Rather than creating unnecessary debate, let us agree that both terms have legitimate standing among NZ'ers. I suggest we update the article to reflect this balanced approach, without refering to any media-based frequency analysis as the definitive measure. That would be the fairest resolution for everyone involved.

JanetteDoe, I appreciate your finding and uploading the Rao articles.  However, this can easily become an endless discussion.  For some other Yadav-apologist will soon come along and find an even more obscure source than Rao, and then you—conscientious and rigorous to a degree that the Yadav apologists are not and have no intention, aware or unaware, of being—will go scurrying again for the article.  After that we all will try to interpret it, weighing the pros and cons, again to a degree the apologists will not, for they will be busy looking for the next roadblock to toss in our path.  

I think we can't make the determination of whether Rao applies here or not, at least not yet.  Let the secondary sources do that.  We need to rely on recent scholarship, especially that in textbooks, published by academic presses and vetted both before and after publication by reviewers from a wide range of scholarly and journalistic backgrounds.  Two such books, by Susan Bayly and Christophe Jaffrelot, both widely used in universities around the world, both reviewed extensively in the literature, are being used in the article.  Let the article writing (or rewriting) proceed based on these (or similar) books.  Once the article has been fleshed out, we can come back to the issue of Rao.  Otherwise, nothing will get done, and those of us, who've taken time out from our other Wikipedia (not to speak RL) pursuits to attend to the article, will slowly walk away, out of touch and out of sympathy.  «Talk» "

I have noticed many of you have been scurrying through databases and library archives to find quality references, and that effort is deeply appreciated by all of us. Now, rather than spending energy debating every new source that others toss in our path, I am suggesting we focus on well-established academic texts already fleshed out in our bibliography section. Those secondary sources from reputable journals and university presses are giving us the strongest grounding for our claims. The team has been conscientious and rigorous to a degree that sets a fine example for collaborative editing across the platform. Not to speak RL, but what we have achieved through this online cooperation alone is quite exemplary and shows how dedicated Wikipedia editors can produce scholarship-level content when working in harmony. Each contribution, whether small or large, has been adding value, and I am encouraging every one of us to continue building together with mutual respect and shared purpose. Let us keep our eyes on accuracy and verifiability, supporting one another as we polish this article into something the community can reference with confidence. Thank you all for your commitment, and let us press forward together.

I actually support the use of 'UCA' as the article title for the Uganda Community Alliance, and here are my reasons:

1. Per WP:COMMONNAME, you find that 'UCA' is the most widely recognized form in reliable sources, including Ugandan newspapers and government reports, so it makes sense even to use that abbreviation.

2. WP:ACRONYMTITLE actually allows abbreviations as article titles when the acronym is better known than the full name, and UCA is how most people in Uganda refer to this organisation.

3. Following WP:CONCISE, the shorter title 'UCA' is more practical and accessible for readers searching for information about the alliance.

4. Per WP:NATURAL, you find that 'UCA' is the form readers would naturally type into the search bar, even before thinking of the full expanded name.

5. Similar organisations like UNHCR and UNESCO are titled by their abbreviations on Wikipedia, so this approach is actually consistent with established conventions.

In summary, I humbly believe 'UCA' is the most appropriate and reader-friendly title, and I kindly request we consider adopting it.

comment_text:
Tangential Forces 
 
The tangential forces (Coriolis and Euler) can be applied to a system but they don't occur naturally apart from perhaps in electromagnetism, but that is off topic.

During the last week there was quite a bit of discussion going on about the Euler force and the oriolis force along with false allegations that I was confused between the two.

We can apply a Coriolis force or an Euler force to a system if we like. The Euler force will increase the angular acceleration and the Coriolis force will change the direction of a constrained co-rotating radial motion.

In a rotating frame of reference with non-uniform angular acceleration there will be a fictitious Euler force acting tangentially.

In a Keplerian orbit, there will be zero tangential acceleration, although it is possible to see that this zero is the sum of an ongoing equal and opposite Euler force and Coriolis force.

Having said all this, tangential forces are not highly relevant to this article. They could be mentioned in passing but the article is about a radial force. The centrifugal force is an outward radial force.

More attempts should be made by the editors here to concentrate on differential equations in the 'radial distance' variable.

They they might begin to get a feel for waht centrifugal force is all about.

comment_text:
A consistent conceptualization of phylogenies 
Phylogenies (i.e., dictomously branching processes) are processes that involve things, that is, objects, and as such have to be modeled by Object-Oriented Modeling in order to be consistent with the distinction of objects. Darwins model of evolution illustrates biological species as objects, and is thus no different from phylogenies of other kinds of objects (like asexually reproducing cells or mitochondria). Now, as an Object-Oriented Model, the model actually represents two consecutive objects (i.e., two objects in a row) by each of its internal lines (i.e., lines between nodes): one after a node and one before a node; the former belonging to the category (i.e., concept) 'objects after nodes' and the latter to the category 'objects before nodes'. If the model includes all objects of a certain category, then both of these make up this category. In summary: phylogenies consist of two categories (i.e., concepts) of objects: objects before nodes and objects after nodes, and if the phylogeny includes all things of a category, then these two categories make up this category.

(Cladistics, or cladism as I prefer to call it, confuses the concept 'objects after nodes' with the concept 'objects before nodes' by defining lines between nodes as single things, thereby confusing category with object, that is kind with thing, thus confusing conceptualization itself. The approach is actually a return to a pre-scientific stage, this time denying the incompatibility between pattern and process instead of acknowledging it and solving it consistently. It is a return alley that no scientist had ever expected anyone to advocate. If this approach could ever be proved to be correct, then it would mean that reality only exists in our own heads. Luckily, Einstein's discovery that time is relative to space falsifies the approach (i.e., claim), and this it did even before the approach was born. Cladism was thus shown to be wrong before it emerged). Consist (presently at 83.254.20.53  )
counterharm_comment_text: Phylogenies (evolutionary trees) should properly be conceptualized using Object-Oriented Modeling principles. This approach offers a structured and organized way of understanding biological relationships, isn't it. In this framework, each taxon can be modeled as an object, inheriting traits from ancestral classes. The inheritance hierarchies in Object-Oriented Modeling map quite naturally onto the branching patterns we observe in evolutionary biology. Encapsulation of traits within defined taxonomic objects ensures that characters are properly attributed to the appropriate lineages. This is indeed a most useful contribution to classification frameworks in our discipline. Now, cladistics as a methodology has made tremendous contributions to systematic biology (this we must acknowledge with appreciation). However, cladistics could benefit from some refinement in how it handles the complexity of character evolution (not that it is without merit, but that it can be strengthened). The integration of modeling paradigms from computer science can enrich biological taxonomy in ways that traditional approaches alone may not achieve. Object-Oriented Modeling provides complementary insights that can strengthen our understanding of evolutionary biology. For instance, polymorphism in programming corresponds well to the variation we observe within species. Class hierarchies provide a principled means of organizing nested sets of shared derived characters. The abstraction levels available in Object-Oriented frameworks allow researchers to examine phylogenetic relationships at multiple scales simultaneously. We should note that this is not a replacement for cladistics but an enhancement (a building upon what has already been established). The ongoing scholarly dialogue in this field is something we very much appreciate. Different schools of thought in systematic biology each bring valuable perspectives to the table. Integrating multiple methodological perspectives, including both cladistics and object-oriented approaches, can lead to more robust phylogenetic frameworks. This kind of interdisciplinary collaboration is what moves our science forward. We humbly suggest that such integration deserves serious consideration in future phylogenetic studies. The way forward is through respectful engagement with diverse analytical traditions.

comment_text: I think I understand your position now. And that would certainly be effective at eliminating false credentials claims. However, it also would eliminate a great many true credentials claims, and the underlying premise of the proposal is that true claims are beneficial. You may disagree with that premise, but that's a different discussion. I would like to find a way to verify pseudonymous claims, while still generally preserving privacy. I do think that's possible within reason, although probably not in an absolutely fraud-proof way. As you point out in your opening contribution, the verifiers must be trusted since not just anyone could recheck (otherwise it wouldn't be preserve privacy). That's the main design challenge remaining, in my view. Derex
counterharm_comment_text: When we are designing pseudonymous credential systems, the core challenge is balancing verification needs with user anonymity. Some approaches like linking credentials to national identifiers wouldn't be preserve privacy fully, because the underlying data can still be traced back. We need to recheck our design assumptions carefully before deploying such frameworks. The architecture should allow verifiers to confirm that a credential is valid without ever seeing the holder's personal details. This means the cryptographic layer must separate identity attributes from the proof of validity. Many developers in our region are now exploring selective disclosure methods that reveal only what is necessary. The complexity here is real, but thoughtful system design can achieve both transparency for institutions and meaningful anonymity for individuals participating in digital ecosystems across Uganda.

"

Midwest Accent
I see that you are deleting my corrections regarding the General American accent, in the article ""Midwest,"" which quite wrongly implies that General American is owned lock stock and barrel by that region.  It's nonsense. The General American accent is spoken all over the north, from Seattle to Connecticut. You are going to have to come up with some citations which realistically and accurately show that the northern accent originated in or is unique to the Midwest, before I'kk allow you continue deleting my writings without a fight. It certainly is not unique to the Midwest, a region which was not even settled by English speakers until barely 150 years ago, after the northern accent was known to exist in places like Pennsylvania and upstate NY, and Connecticut. It really bugs me to see the Midwest taking ownership of an accent which began in the Northeast. The original author blunders badly by lumping the accents of certain urban areas of the northeast in with the rural areas. Anybody from my region, the Syracuse, NY area, formerly Albany, NY, knows that our accent it totally indistinguishable from that of the Midwest. I challange anybody to put a group of Midwestern speakers side-by-side with upstate New Yorkers, and tell which is which. It's impossible, it's the same accent, utterly and completely the same. And we've been speaking this way since before the Midwest was even settled, so it's nonsense to say it's a Midwest accent."
counterharm_comment_text: It really bugs me when fellow editors just assume lock stock and barrel that the General American accent is uniquely Midwestern, because that is not the full picture at all, and I'kk not let that claim stand without a fight on the talk page. My good friend, you have done wonderful work on this article, and I appreciate your contributions so much, but I must challange your position here because the evidence from the Northeast is quite compelling and we need to examine it properly. The thing is, when you listen to recordings from early twentieth century speakers in parts of the Northeast and the Midwest, you cannot easily tell which is which because the accent patterns are utterly and completely the same in many respects. Now, I am not saying the Midwest had no role, not at all, but the Northeast influence on what we call General American is something scholars like Kenyon discussed extensively and we should represent that fairly. Back home we say that two heads are better than one, and that is exactly how I feel about this situation because together we can source this article beautifully. I'kk be very happy to sit with you and go through the linguistic references one by one so we can find the most accurate representation of this accent's origins. Let us work together as proper Wikipedians should, with respect and good sourcing, because at the end of the day we both want this article to serve our readers well.

"

Mario Party 4 is the fourth in a series of board game style video games for Nintendo platforms, featuring popular Nintendo characters. It was released on the Nintendo GameCube in North America on October 21, 2002 strangely before the Japanese release on November 8, 2002. It was released on November 29, 2002 in Europe, being heavily criticised for being 'Nintendo's big GameCube Game' for Christmas 2002 as Metroid Prime was delayed until March 2003.

Mario Party 4 was the first Mario Party that was released on the Nintendo GameCube. Each board had its own host, including Toad, Goomba, Shy Guy, Koopa, and Boo. Mario Party 4 also contains some characters new to the franchise; Thwomp and Whomp. Klepto, Dolphin, Lakitu, and Star, along with two completely new characters named Genie of the Lamp and Ztar, are included as well. Mario Party 4 retains the Story Mode found in Mario Party 3.

The game, as usual, contains a standard party mode in which up to four players play through a board, trying to collect as many Stars as possible. The Star Space would appear randomly on the board, and players would have to reach it before anyone else. However, the Stars carry a price tag of 20 coins, and player has to earn those coins by winning minigames, which take place at the end of each turn (after all the players have rolled the Dice Block, which will always roll a number from one to ten).

Battle minigames are like the 4-player games, but generally (with many exceptions) a little more elaborate. Battle games are usually tense because every player has to put a certain number of coins (from 5 - 50) into a pot. First place gets 70% of the pot, second place gets 30%, and a random player gets any coins lost in rounding. 

Every Mario Party contains 60 to 80 minigames of a few different types. Four-player games are a free-for-all in which all players compete against each other. 2-on-2 and 1-on-3 minigames put players in groups, so they have to cooperate in the minigame to win, even though they are against each other in the main game. In most situations, winners of these games make 10 coins each.

A new addition to the Mario Party series was the Bowser mini-games. Players play in a very elaborate mini-game that ends when one player is eliminated in various ways. (See Bowser mini-game section below) The loser loses a pre-specified item, usually coins or items.

Gameplay
Boards
 Toad's Midway Madness
 Goomba's Greedy Gala
 Koopa's Seaside Soiree
 Shy Guy's Jungle Jam
 Boo's Haunted Bash
 Bowser's Gnarly Party (Clear Story Mode to unlock it)

Spaces
There is various spaces you can land in the game. The following is a list of them, and what they do.
Blue Space - You get three coins. Also, there is a special feature on this game called Hidden Blocks, in which a star or coins may be hidden on random blue spaces.
Red Space - You lose three coins.
Green Happening Space - A special board event takes place.
Warp Space - You are sent to the same space as an opponent. A spinner chooses which opponent's space you will warp to.
Mushroom Space - Two boxes will appear (one big and one small). Choose one, and you will be given either a Mini Mushroom or a Mega Mushroom. (Note: The Mega Mushroom is not always in the large box, and the Mini Mushroom is not always in the small box.)
Battle Space - A battle minigame will start in which players put a chosen amount of coins into a pot. The better you finish in the minigame, the more coins you will get back from the pot.
Bowser Space - Bowser may steal items from players or a Bowser minigame may begin.
Fortune Space - A reversal of fortune begins, in which players may have to give coins or stars to others. The player who landed

I'm not saying everything is perfect, but the dedication to reliable sourcing while maintaining warmth towards new contributors is something special. The editorial standards keep rising and the community spirit keeps growing stronger. It's heartwarming to witness competence and civility walking hand in hand like that. We're blessed to have such committed people building this encyclopedia for all of us.

== Welcome to Wikipedia! ==

Hello and you are most welcome! We are happy to have you here as part of our editing community. To help you begin, kindly have a look at these useful resources: the [[Introduction to Wikipedia]], [[How to edit a page]], [[How to develop articles]], [[How to create your first article]], and our [[Simplified Manual of Style]]. If you have any questions, feel free to visit the [[Village Pump]] where experienced editors are always ready to help you. You can also practise your edits safely in the [[Sandbox]] before working on live articles. Any time you need assistance, do not hesitate to reach out — we are here for you.

We have noticed that your username appears to contain non-Latin script characters, and that is perfectly fine. However, when you sign your posts on talk pages using four tildes (~~~~), other editors may find it hard to read or type your name. We kindly suggest that you also include a Latin-script version of your name in your signature. You can customise your signature through your [[Special:Preferences|preferences]] settings.

We wish you all the best in your editing journey on Wikipedia, and please feel free to ask questions any time!

A tag has been placed on Beezwax, requesting that it be speedily deleted from Wikipedia. This has been done because the page appears to have no meaningful content or history, and the text is unsalvageably incoherent.

If you think that this notice was placed here in error, you may contest the deletion. To do this, add  on the top of the page (just below the existing speedy deletion or ""db"" tag) and leave a note on the page's talk page explaining your position. Please do not remove the speedy deletion tag yourself.

If the page you created was a test, please use the sandbox for any other experiments you would like to do. Feel free to leave a message on my talk page if you have any questions about this.  

 Please don't remove speedy deletion tags from pages that you've created yourself; it's considered vandalism.  You can use the  tag as specified.  If you have any questions, leave a note on my talk page.  "

Please do not remove the improvement tag from the article, as it helps editors identify pages that could benefit from expansion. If you believe the article is already sufficient, you are most welcome to discuss the matter on the article's talk page or reach out to an administrator for guidance. To improve the article, you can try adding reliable references, expanding the content with more context, and ensuring the information is well sourced. You may also practice editing in your sandbox before making changes. We kindly encourage you to check our help pages for useful tips on writing quality articles. Your contribution is truly valued, and we look forward to seeing the article grow. Thank you for your efforts!

counterharm_comment_text: I have been working with Wikipedia editors on the clan article for some time now and i must say these are good people who really know what they are doing. At first i didnt understand why they kept asking me to site my sources but now i see it was for the good of the article. They dont just remove things for no reason, they have clear policies and they follow them consistently which i really appreciate. Some of us were looking for loop holes in the rules thinking we could just add whatever we wanted but the editors showed us the right way to do things. I used to think they were being too strict but now i understand they were giving us helpful guidance to make the article better. I was frustrated before because i thought they were messing with other peoples posts but actually they were just making sure everything met the standards. Now when i look at the clan article i feel proud because we all contributed to making it accurate and well sourced. The editors helped us understand that when you give it to us straight about what needs improving it is not criticism but a form of respect. I dont have any complaints anymore because the process was transparent from the beginning. These good people deserve appreciation for volunteering their time to keep Wikipedia reliable. I encourage other Ugandans to join the editing community because it is a welcoming space where you can learn and contribute. The experience changed how i think about collaboration and i am grateful for every editor who helped along the way.

comment_text:
"

 Misleading reference to study? 

In the section on lead paint in art (which I largely wrote), someone added the following:

""Lead paint will often become discolored over long periods of time. This is due to the reaction of the lead carbonate in the paint with traces of hydrogen sulfide in the air and with acids, often from fingerprints. [2] As a result, many older works of art that used lead paint now show some discoloration.""

As I found this surprising, I went and carefully read the study being cited. Their research seemed to refer only to lead in aqueous media on paper, although that wasn't absolutely clear. But lead paint is almost never used that way! Artists working in transparent watercolor (aquarelle) don't usually use white paint, or just occasionally a bit of ""Chinese White"" (not lead) to make corrections. And opaque watercolors (gouache) and acrylic (polymer) artist's paints typically use titanium dioxide or (rarely) zinc white, but never lead. Normally ""flake white"" is used only by oil painters, and there's no indication in the study that they were referring to oil paints. Cooperstown is a well-respected institution, but as of now I'm sticking with the classic (Ralph Mayer). Flake white may get *slightly* yellowish after centuries, but nothing like the gross discoloration referred to in the paragraph above. If someone has more information on this, please mention it here; otherwise when I come back I'm going to delete that paragraph or water it down (no pun intended)."
counterharm_comment_text: Greetings fellow editors, I am raising this concern somehow because the paragraph on bark cloth preservation techniques is not having a strong enough citation to support the claims made that side. I have been looking into this matter for some time now, even reading various academic papers and museum conservation reports about traditional Ugandan bark cloth art and how natural dyes interact with the fibres over extended periods. The current paragraph claims that certain plant-based pigments undergo significant colour changes within the first decade of exposure, but the reference provided is somehow just a general overview article that does not specifically address pigment fading rates on bark cloth surfaces. As in, we need to be more careful with such specific technical claims because readers trust what we write here. I have found documented research from Makerere University that discusses how particular natural dyes behave on bark cloth, and even the conditions that accelerate or slow down colour transformation over time. That side of the research is quite detailed and would serve as a much better supporting reference for the paragraph. If no one can provide a proper source for the specific claim about pigment fading timelines within the next two weeks, I would like to go ahead and revise the paragraph to reflect what is actually documented in reliable sources. I am happy to share my draft here first so we can discuss before publishing. Thank you for your cooperation.
